data.title: US military conducts first nuclear microreactor air transport
data.description:
The Pentagon, along with the Department of Energy, on Sunday airlifted a small nuclear reactor, the first such transportation as the Trump administration looks to quickly deploy nuclear power across the U.S.

The agencies moved the reactor in partnership with Valar Atomics, flying parts of one of the firm’s unfueled Ward 250 microreactors on a C-17 aircraft from March Air Reserve Base, Calif., to Hill Air Force Base, Utah.

Under Secretary of Defense for acquisition and sustainment Michael Duffey and Energy Secretary Chris Wright were aboard the flight and lauded the event as the start of the “American nuclear renaissance.”

“Powering next generation warfare will require us to move faster than our adversaries, to build a system that doesn’t just equip our warfighters to fight, but equips them to win at extraordinary speed,” Duffey said. “Today is a monumental step toward building that system.”

He said that such a reactor could provide energy security on a military base without the need for the civilian power grid, and in military operations overseas such reactors “would mean U.S. forces could operate without concern that an enemy might cut fuel supplies.” 

The Ward 250 is a 5 megawatt nuclear reactor that could potentially power roughly 5,000 homes, according to the Pentagon. 

President Trump has pushed for the U.S. to expand its energy production and pledged to get three microreactors “critical” — or running all of its nuclear systems smoothly — on U.S. soil by July 4, Wright said.

As part of that effort, Trump last May issued four executive orders meant to expand domestic nuclear deployment to meet growing energy demands in national security AI data centers, and space and cyber infrastructure. 

Valar Atomics in August announced it had been selected by the Energy Department to achieve criticality as part of one of the executive orders, and is looking to sell power on a test basis in 2027, before becoming fully commercial in 2028.

“The American nuclear renaissance is to get that ball moving again, fast, carefully, but with private capital, American innovation and determination,” Wright said. 

Proponents of microreactors say they are easily transported energy sources that can be sent to remote locations and don’t require frequent deliveries of fuel compared to generators. 

But others argue that even small nuclear reactors produce a significant amount of radioactive waste — the disposal of which is an unresolved issue — and can’t yet generate power for a reasonable price. 

The reactor will eventually head to the Utah San Rafael Energy Lab for testing and evaluation, according to the Pentagon.

data.title: Witch hunt alert! Epstein co-conspirators revealed as … random guys?
data.description:
Frankly, I didn’t expect to be proven right this quickly. But here we are. 

Remember just two weeks ago, when I warned that releasing all of the Epstein files, including not just emails but also uncorroborated reports and investigative documents, was an unprecedented course of action that would end up exposing innocent people to humiliation and retaliation? 

Well, guess what. 

We learned on Friday that four of those six names uttered by Rep. Ro Khanna on the House floor last week — the names of alleged Epstein co-conspirators whom Rep. Thomas Massie said were “likely incriminated” — are just four random people. They have nothing whatsoever to do with Epstein.

How is that possible? Let me explain.  

As part of their investigation, law enforcement with the Southern District of New York assembled a photo lineup of potential suspects. If you’ve watched police procedurals on television, you probably know how that goes. A witness is given a bunch of photos, only one of them is the suspect, and the investigators see if the witness identifies the correct one. The other photos depict people who have nothing to do with the crime. 

And that was the case here, as Khanna himself now concedes.  

The congressman writes on X: “I appreciate @JSweetLI’s reporting confirming today that Salvatore Nuara, Zurab Mikeladze, Leonid Leonov, and Nicola Caputo were just part of a photo line up and are not connected to Epstein’s crimes. 

I wish DOJ had provided that explanation earlier instead of redacting then unredacting their names. They have failed to protect survivors, created confusion for innocent men, and have protected rich and powerful abusers. 

We must have full transparency and the truth.” 

In all fairness to the congressmen, Massie did state previously that one reason those four names might have been redacted was specifically because they appeared in a police lineup. And it turns out he was right about that. But I think, given this possibility, it was unwise for Khanna to take to the House floor and smear four random people as criminal co-conspirators in a global sex trafficking ring. 

By the way, this is exactly why it is not common to release investigative files of this nature — they contain information that investigators gathered, for whatever reason, that may have nothing to do with the case.  

It’s why Rep. Clay Higgins of Florida, the lone no vote on the Epstein Files Transparency Act, was 1,000 percent correct when he said: “It abandons 250 years of criminal justice procedure in America. As written, this bill reveals and injures thousands of innocent people – witnesses, people who provided alibis, family members, etc. If enacted in its current form, this type of broad reveal of criminal investigative files, released to a rabid media, will absolutely result in innocent people being hurt.” 

Higgins was right from the start. Now, I can’t claim that I came to my senses that quickly, I admit it. But it looks like I got there a little bit quicker than some other folks. To all the people who’ve been screaming at me online that I’m just running cover for elite sexual abusers, or I don’t care about victims, or I’m in league with Israeli intelligence, or whatever the ridiculous smear of the moment is …. it’s never too late to put down the pitchforks. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Pentagon reviewing Anthropic partnership over terms of use dispute
data.description:
The Pentagon is reviewing its relationship with artificial intelligence (AI) giant Anthropic over the terms of use of its AI model, which was used by the U.S. military during last month’s operation to capture Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro.  

“The Department of War’s relationship with Anthropic is being reviewed. Our nation requires that our partners be willing to help our warfighters win in any fight. Ultimately, this is about our troops and the safety of the American people,” chief Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell told The Hill in a statement on Monday. 

For months, the Pentagon and Anthropic have held discussions around the U.S. military’s terms of use of Claude, its signature AI product.

But now the Pentagon is close to cutting ties with Anthropic and labeling the San Francisco-based company as a supply chain risk, Axios reported Monday. The company wants to make sure that its tools are not used to develop weaponry that fires without human input and that its products are not used for mass surveillance on Americans. 

Defense Department officials have argued that those terms would confine the U.S. military and make it more difficult to work under such conditions, the outlet noted.

The tensions were highlighted by the Pentagon’s Venezuela operations, which reportedly prompted Anthropic to inquire about whether its technology was used in the raid. The Wall Street Journal reported last week that Claude was used through Anthropic’s partnership with Palantir, which has extensive military contracts.

Anthropic’s contract with the Pentagon is valued at up to $200 million and was announced last July. 

Anthropic said it is “committed” to using frontier AI to support U.S. national security. 

“That’s why we were the first frontier AI company to put our models on classified networks and the first to provide customized models for national security customers. Claude is used for a wide variety of intelligence-related use cases across the government, including the DoW, in line with our Usage Policy,” an Anthropic spokesperson told The Hill in a statement on Monday. 

“We are having productive conversations, in good faith, with DoW on how to continue that work and get these complex issues right,” the spokesperson added.

data.title: US diplomatic overload is at crisis levels
data.description:
The U.S. is facing a diplomatic overload crisis on steroids. Worse, the Trump administration is relying on a single, highly inexperienced team to oversee three important negotiations. Thus far, it is unclear if this team or a more experienced one deal with what could be the most treacherous of all.

Son-in-law Jared Kushner and crypto-bitcoin partner Steve Witkoff have been leading the talks over Gaza, Ukraine and now with Iran. Before working with the Trump administration, each had no prior experience as diplomats. And to any reasonable observer, both are deeply conflicted, with outside business interests that would normally be disqualifying.

Kushner is managing some $2 billion for Saudi Arabia’s sovereign wealth fund. It is possible that Witkoff could profit from a deal with Ukraine on rare earth minerals as part of joint ventures, as Kushner has in the Gulf.

The president believes that he has brought peace to the Middle East and Gaza. Israel’s brutal war against Hamas has subsided, but Hamas still exists. Most of Gaza is in ruins.  resembling the bombed out cities of Berlin and Hamburg during World War II. And Gazans face dire economic, sanitation and other conditions, many of which are life threatening. Thus far, Trump’s broader plans for a Gaza Board of Peace remain moribund.

Ukraine is still very much at war with Russia. Reports of “1.2 million casualties (killed, wounded, and missing) and as many as 325,000 killed since February 2022” seem realistic. A cynic has observed that a snail would have covered more ground than the Russians have been able to achieve since their invasion four years ago. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky complained that the U.S. has discussed secret business deals with Russia, so far unconfirmed by the White House.

Meanwhile, the Kushner-Witkoff team has shifted to negotiations with Tehran. The Abraham Lincoln Carrier Strike Group is in proximity in the Arabian Gulf, and the U.S. has reportedly increased its air and missile defense forces in the Gulf states. A second carrier strike group may be on the way to exert further leverage on the mullahs to reach some sort of agreement. The question is on what?

The first Trump administration cancelled the Obama administration’s Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action with Iran. If Iran had abided by the treaty and did not violate it, Tehran would never have built nuclear weapons. At the time, the International Atomic Energy Agency had sufficient surveillance systems in place to verify compliance and prevent Iran from cheating. That no longer exists.

The Iran negotiation is more complicated than just nuclear weapons. The U.S. believes Iran is the world’s single largest supporter of terrorism given how it has underwritten Hamas and Hezbollah. It also has thousands of long and short-range missiles that threaten the Gulf states and, from the U.S. perspective, more importantly, Israel.  

The U.S. also demands that the clerical oligarchy cease and desist killing Iranian protestors and stop any and all violence against its citizens. Obviously, the presence of at least one carrier strike group is meant to intimidate the mullahs into some form of compromise.

Thus far, despite the president’s optimism over the direction the talks are headed, what are the feasible outcomes, given Iran’s adamant stand about retaining the capacity for pursuing nuclear energy? The mullahs are unlikely to give up their missiles that are viewed as critical for self-defense. While Hamas and Hezbollah have been seriously degraded in Gaza, Lebanon and Syria, both still exist. So what can be achieved by talks with Iran?

The fourth and last is what, if anything, will take the place of the now-expired New START Treaty that limited both the U.S. and Russia to 1,550 deployable warheads and 700 launchers?  The U.S. has a total inventory of about 5,200 nuclear and thermonuclear weapons; the Russians several hundred more. China is increasing its nuclear weapons capacity, some argue rapidly.

And there is the ugly question of future and further proliferation. Britain, France, India, Israel, Pakistan and North Korea are nuclear weapons states. If, as the U.S. states, Europe must become more responsible for its defense, does this suggest a possible nuclear arms race? Germany, Poland, Sweden and Ukraine have the technical capacity to make these weapons.

As China expands its nuclear arsenal, Japan, South Korea and Taiwan could possibly follow suit, as could Australia. Who is concerned about this? With American diplomatic overstretch, can the nation carry out four successful negotiations at once? Unfortunately, it’s not likely.

Harlan Ullman, Ph.D., is UPI’s Arnaud deBorchgrave Distinguished Columnist, a senior advisor at the Atlantic Council, the chairman of two private companies and the principal author of the doctrine of shock and awe. He and former United Kingdom Defense Chief David Richards are the authors of a forthcoming book on preventing strategic catastrophe.

data.title: Trump says Guthrie kidnappers will face death penalty if she is killed
data.description:
President Trump said Monday he would direct the Justice Department to seek the death penalty for kidnappers of Nancy Guthrie if the 84-year-old mother of “Today” show co-anchor Savannah Guthrie is found dead.

In a brief phone interview with the New York Post, the president said consequences for the kidnappers would be “very, very severe — the most severe” if they kill the older Guthrie, who was abducted from her Arizona home just more than two weeks ago.

When pressed on whether the Justice Department would request the death penalty in the case, Trump told the Post, “The most, yeah — that’s true.”

Guthrie was last seen on the night of Jan. 31 at her home outside of Tucson, Ariz. She was reported missing the following day, and investigators believe she was taken against her will. 

The FBI is offering a reward of up to $100,000 for information leading to the location of her and/or the arrest and conviction of anyone involved in her disappearance. The bureau is describing a suspect in the investigation, captured by the 84-year-old’s doorbell camera, as a man standing between 5-foot-9 and 5-foot-10 with an average build. 

In the surveillance images and videos the FBI released last week, the individual is donning dark gloves, sweatpants and a jacket. The bureau also said Thursday he was carrying a black, 25-liter Ozark Trail Hiker Pack backpack. 

Trump called Savannah Guthrie on Feb. 4 to offer support to the longtime journalist and said he directed “ALL Federal Law Enforcement to be at the family’s, and Local Law Enforcement’s, complete disposal, IMMEDIATELY. We are deploying all resources to get her mother home safely.”

On Friday, Trump said he thinks “progress has been made” in the investigation. The younger Guthrie released a video Sunday asking those with information to come forward, saying, “it is never too late to do the right thing, and we are here.”

data.title: Congress launched our nation. It must embrace fact-based representation.
data.description:
Presidents’ Day — or as the federal government designates it, George Washington’s Birthday — occurs each year on the third Monday in February and is understood by many as an occasion to celebrate the contributions of Washington, our founding president, and Abraham Lincoln, who is widely believed to have been America’s greatest president.

In 2026, Presidents’ Day takes on added meaning because it is the year when our country celebrates the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence and the launch of the American democratic experiment.

But the attention we give to our presidents overshadows a critical fact about our country’s origin story and the structure of our government. It was Congress that issued the Declaration of Independence, founded our country, and funded and oversaw the Revolutionary War. And it was Congress that ran our country until the ratification of the U.S. Constitution in 1789, a document that unambiguously grants most of the federal government’s powers to Congress.

This was no accident. The American Revolution was a reaction against the tyranny that results when one person holds too much power and people are denied a voice in decisions that determine their rights and opportunities. More than 50 percent of the Declaration’s words are devoted to listing the many “injuries and usurpations” committed by King George against the American colonies, including disabling or shutting down the colonies’ elected legislatures. The colonists were also furious at the British Parliament for imposing taxes and enacting other laws that disregarded the policies and authority of the colonies’ elected assemblies.

To counter tyranny, Americans sent a group of representatives — chosen by their elected colonial assemblies — to meet in a Continental Congress, to unite the diverse and fractious colonies, and to make a new country based on the promise of representation. Representation — having a voice — is our country’s defining promise.

If Americans want to preserve that promise, we must demand fact-based representation from the people we elect to serve us in Congress. And we must demand that Congress fulfill its responsibility under the Constitution to act as a check on the executive branch and against the return of encroaching autocracy.

For example, Congress recently enacted an appropriations law that funds most of the government for the remainder of the fiscal year, and it demonstrates what Congress can do to counter a president’s autocratic impulses. The law reverses a host of Trump administration policies, including its efforts to decimate scientific research and the Department of Education, personnel reductions in numerous federal agencies, and hits to everything from refugee assistance to food stamps.

But like all laws, its impact is only as great as our ability to oversee its execution. Information is power. Now Congress must demand information to ensure that President Trump and his administration — as Article II, Section 3 of the Constitution commands — “take Care that the Laws be faithfully executed.” That is a solemn charge. It can only happen if Republicans and Democrats in Congress bring even greater unity and tenacity to overseeing the budget law’s implementation than they have shown in other cases, such as demanding transparency regarding the Jeffrey Epstein files.

The Trump administration has refused on numerous occasions to share information with Congress and the public about its initiatives and has been dismissive about congressional power, but recent events demonstrate the power Congress has under the Constitution if it chooses to use it. With the fiscal 2027 budget season already upon us, members of Congress will have a fresh opportunity to stand up for their institution and the people who elected them by demanding the information they require to fulfill their constitutional duties and rejecting budgets and policies from the administration that are harmful to the public.

Congress, through its upcoming hearings in the budget and appropriations committees, can demand information about the administration’s adherence to the fiscal 2026 law. As Vanderbilt law professors Kevin Stack and Michael Vandenbergh have written, if Trump and company refuse to cooperate with these information requests, Congress can draw on a number of tools to force the administration to comply — including docking recalcitrant administrators’ pay and denying rogue agencies the funds used to obstruct congressional inquiries.

The Constitution gives our directly-elected representatives the most power because they are — or should be — the most accountable to us. We should remember that, not only on Presidents’ Day but every day, and urge every member of Congress to fulfill the values of representation that fueled our revolution and our march toward democracy.

Jim Townsend is director of the Carl Levin Center for Oversight and Democracy. The Levin Center is based at Wayne State University Law School in Detroit and has an office in Washington, D.C. The views expressed here are solely those of the Levin Center and do not necessarily reflect the position of Wayne State University or the Law School.

data.title: Iranian navy launches military exercises ahead of nuclear talks
data.description:
Iranian naval forces began military exercises Monday in the strategic waterway of the Strait of Hormuz, the day before a second round of talks with the United States for a nuclear deal amid heightened tensions. 

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps’s naval drills are dubbed “Smart Control of Hormuz Strait” and are rehearsing scenarios to respond to potential security and military threats in the Strait of Hormuz region, Iranian state media reported. 

The U.S. is preparing for a weeks-long military operation against Iran if President Trump orders an attack on the country, Reuters reported.

The Strait of Hormuz is a key waterway that lies between Oman and Iran, connecting the Persian Gulf with the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea. It is considered one of the most important oil transit arteries, with a fifth of the world’s total oil consumption passing through the strait. 

Trump has dispatched two aircraft carriers to the region amid renewed nuclear talks with Iran, flexing America’s military might over Tehran as pressure to come to a deal — less than a year after U.S.-Israeli strikes against Iranian nuclear facilities in June. 

Trump is looking to forge a new deal with Iran limiting its nuclear capabilities after withdrawing in 2018 from the Obama administration’s nuclear deal, the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action, which imposed limits on Iran’s enrichment of uranium in exchange for sanctions relief.

It’s not clear where U.S. officials will land on Iran being able to enrich uranium, which Tehran claims is only for peaceful purposes. But the Trump administration has said Iran’s ballistic missile program and support for terrorist proxies must also be part of the talks, which Iran has rejected. 

“It’s been very difficult for anyone to do real deals with Iran, because we’re dealing with radical Shia clerics who are making theological decisions, not geopolitical ones,” Secretary of State Marco Rubio told reporters Monday, while on diplomatic travel in Hungary.

“But let’s see what happens. I hope it works out.”

A U.S. delegation including Trump’s special envoy Steve Witkoff and son-in-law Jared Kushner will hold talks in Geneva on Tuesday with Iranian officials, marking the second meeting following a six-hour round of talks in Oman earlier this month. Witkoff and Kushner are also expected to participate in a separate set of talks, between Ukraine and Russia, Tuesday afternoon in Geneva. 

Ahead of the meeting with the U.S., Iran’s Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi on Monday met with Rafael Grossi, the head of the United Nations nuclear watchdog, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). 

Grossi said in a statement on social platform X that he held “in-depth technical discussions” with Araghchi “in preparation for important negotiations scheduled for tomorrow in Geneva.”

Araghchi said in a post on X that the talks with Grossi centered on technical issues related to Iran-IAEA cooperation, which has been strained since the U.S. bombing last year.

An Iranian diplomat reportedly said Sunday that a potential deal with the U.S. could result in economic deals and benefits for both countries. 

“Common interests in the oil and gas fields, joint fields, mining investments, and even aircraft purchases are included in the negotiations,” said Hamid Ghanbari, foreign ministry deputy director for economic diplomacy, according to the semiofficial Fars news agency.

data.title: FBI not sharing evidence in Pretti shooting, Minnesota authorities say
data.description:
The Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension (BCA) said Monday it was formally notified by the FBI that the bureau will not share any information about its investigation into the death of Alex Pretti.

“The FBI formally notified the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension (BCA) on Feb. 13 that it will not provide the BCA with access to any information or evidence that it has collected in the Jan. 24 shooting death of Alex Pretti,” BCA Superintendent Drew Evans said in a statement.

“While this lack of cooperation is concerning and unprecedented, the BCA is committed to thorough, independent and transparent investigations of these incidents, even if hampered by a lack of access to key information and evidence. Our agency has committed to the FBI and Department of Justice that should its stance change we remain willing to share information that we have obtained with that agency and would welcome a joint investigation. We will continue to pursue all legal avenues to gain access to relevant information and evidence.”

The FBI did not immediately respond to request for comment but has previously refused to coordinate with the Minnesota BCA on the earlier death of Renee Good.

Both Minnesotans were killed by Department of Homeland Security immigration agents amid protests of widespread immigration raids in Minneapolis.

Evans said the BCA still hopes to coordinate on the investigation into the killing of Good, as well as Julio Sosa-Celis, another person shot by federal agents.

While the Trump administration has consistently rebuffed requests to coordinate with the state on the shootings, it did open an investigation through the Justice Department’s Civil Rights Division into the death of Pretti, tapping the involvement of a segment of the department that reviews excessive force by police.

data.title: Teddy Roosevelt descendants press senators on public lands
data.description:
Descendants of former President Theodore Roosevelt, known for his conservationism, are pressing senators over public lands in Minnesota.

In a letter from earlier this month obtained by The New York Times and addressed to members of the Senate, the former president’s descendants pressed the lawmakers to not vote for a resolution going back on a Biden administration decision to prevent mining in a wilderness area in Minnesota.

“The purpose of this letter is to strongly recommend all Senators vote against H.J. Res. 140, to ask you to work with President Trump to seek ways to permanently protect the Boundary Waters, and to send a unified message that America is still a land that relentlessly protects its greatest wilderness terrain,” the descendants wrote.

Last month, the House voted to scrap the Biden administration decision on mining. Through a 214-208 vote, the lower chamber backed revoking safeguards for an area close to the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness in Minnesota.

“Because of the foresight of America’s early Republican leaders, the Boundary Waters has risen (as per the Department of the Interior) to become America’s most heavily visited wilderness area. Its greatness goes way beyond Minnesota, it is the fourth pillar of all of America’s greatest continental wilderness terrain,” Roosevelt’s descendants wrote.

While in office, Roosevelt set up 230 million acres of public lands, founded the U.S. Forest Service and helped create multiple national parks.

In a webpage on the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness, the Forest Service describes it as “over one million acres of rugged and remote boreal forest in the northern third of the Superior National Forest in northeastern Minnesota.”

Late last summer, the Trump administration proposed rescinding a Biden administration rule looking to preserve additional public lands instead of opening them for mining, drilling and grazing.

data.title: Utah Republicans say they have support for redistricting ballot proposal
data.description:
Utah’s GOP chair confirmed Sunday that Republicans had clinched enough signatures to have their ballot initiative, which would repeal a 2018 initiative that created an independent redistricting commission in the state, considered for the November election. 

“We are well over 200,000 signatures that have been submitted,” Robert Axson, who is organizing the ballot measure campaign to get rid of Proposition 4 — Utahns for Representative Government — said in a video posted by Utah Republican National Committee member Brad Bonham. “We have met the threshold.”

Utahns for Representative Government is aiming to repeal Proposition 4, a ballot measure that narrowly passed just above 50 percent in 2018 establishing a seven-member independent commission that creates and recommends maps to state lawmakers. 

The deadline to submit signatures was Sunday evening. State officials now have until March 7 to verify the signatures, according to Deseret News.

The Republican-led effort to get rid of Proposition 4 is running parallel to party efforts to challenge a court-ordered congressional map, which offers Democrats a pickup opportunity in November.  

Republicans are challenging the map at both the state and federal levels, which could have implications for whether Republicans will be able to keep a seat within their fold ahead of November as both parties brace for a fierce fight over control of the House majority. 

In a nod to the national implications of the GOP redistricting ballot measure and control over the House, President Trump endorsed the initiative in a Truth Social post last month. 

“Utahns deserve Maps drawn by those they elect, not Rogue Judges or Leftwing Activists who never faced the Voters, and, therefore, I encourage all Patriotic Utahns, Republicans, and MAGA Supporters who love their Great State and Country to sign this initiative, ASAP,” Trump wrote in the post.
